title: This Will Lower Cortisol & Improve Sleep

chunks.title: Impact of Diet on Sleep
chunks.summary: The discussion opens with insights into how various dietary choices, particularly the intake of carbohydrates and protein, can influence sleep quality. The speaker shares personal experiences asserting that adequate nutrition throughout the day stabilizes blood sugar levels at night, leading to better sleep outcomes.
chunks.transcript: What about uh you know obviously you're you're having quite a bit of carbohydrates, quite a bit of fruit, quite a bit of honey. Do you can you have that straight up to bedtime, no problem, or do you still limit before bed? >> I I stop eating probably at least an hour or two before I go to sleep. Although sometimes I mean I can eat a tablespoon of honey before I go to sleep and it doesn't hurt at all. I think there's some anecdotal evidence for people like taking honey right before they go to sleep. >> Yeah. >> And I So for me like sleep is so interesting like what I do in the whole day affects my sleep, right? I'm really intentional about my food and you know I try and get 160 grams of protein per day, but if I have a low protein day, uh I might not sleep as well because I might get a blood sugar dip in the middle of the night. So like getting enough calories, getting enough carbohydrates from good sources during the day, getting enough protein during the day, that all at least subjectively for me stabilizes my blood sugar at night and I sleep better when I get enough. Like so that's that's another

chunks.title: Benefits of Sunlight for Sleep
chunks.summary: The conversation transitions to the effects of outdoor light exposure on sleep, with the speaker noting that time spent outside significantly improves sleep quality. They explore the differences between natural sunlight and indoor lighting, particularly focusing on how the full spectrum of sunlight positively affects the nervous system and sleep patterns.

chunks.title: Concerns about Indoor Lighting
chunks.summary: The speakers express concerns about the detrimental effects of indoor lighting, highlighting that artificial light, especially blue light and flickering LED lights, can disrupt circadian rhythms. They discuss personal sensitivities to flicker and the impact it has on sleep, emphasizing the need for better lighting choices for optimal health.

It's not going to be like incandescent. It's going to be garbage. And I bring blue light blocking glasses. My sleep is still messed up because it's flickering LED light. chunks.title: Role of Incandescent Light
chunks.summary: A detailed analysis of incandescent lighting follows, with speakers explaining how these bulbs provide a more stable light source compared to LEDs. They discuss the benefits of infrared light from incandescent bulbs, linking it to improved health outcomes and sleep quality, while cautioning against the use of flickering LED lights.

chunks.title: Micronutrient and Light Deficiencies
chunks.summary: The speakers argue that many people are deficient not only in micronutrients but also in necessary forms of light, such as infrared and ultraviolet. This segment includes a critique of common dermatological advice against sun exposure, suggesting that a balanced amount of light is essential for overall health and well-being.
